answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Ram" from *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, the figure of Ram is celebrated as a profound symbol of India's (Hind's) spiritual and intellectual heritage. Iqbal portrays him not merely as a mythological hero from the *Ramayana*, but as a "Spiritual Guide" for Hindustan, evoking national pride and elevating Indian thought above even Western philosophy.[^1] Ram is depicted as a "great Lamp of Light" whose legacy makes "the dusk of Hind... than the dawn itself more bright," suggesting a timeless, transcendent wisdom born in India's domain alongside many other enlightened souls.[^2] He is praised for his peerless valor in battle ("courage... peerless shown") and inner purity ("soul... peerless known"), embodying both martial prowess and a "loving fire."[^3]

The poem frames Ram within a broader exaltation of Hindustan's philosophical chalice "crowned" with the "Wine of Truth," which binds even Western thinkers, positioning Ram as its pinnacle.[^4]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ["Ram"](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/17-ram/translation/#p-f8aace).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ["Ram"](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/17-ram/translation/#p-55f8c1).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ["Ram"](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/17-ram/translation/#p-567e82).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [Chapter: "Ram"](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/17-ram/translation/).
